# Identifiability & bad ESS/R-hat: a case study

You have just learned what R-hat and ESS mean. Now let's see what *really bad*
values look like — and understand *why* they occur.

This module uses a deliberately broken version of the jaw-length regression from
Module 8 to illustrate **non-identifiability**: a situation where the data
cannot, even in principle, pin down the individual parameters you have asked for.

## A two-intercept jaw-length model

Recall the jaw-length regression:

$$
\text{Lower\_jaw\_length}_i = \alpha + \beta \times \text{Standard\_length}_i + \varepsilon_i
$$

Now add a second intercept, `alpha2`, that does nothing but shadow the first:

```
model {
  for (i in 1:N) {
    y[i] ~ dnorm(mu[i], sigma)
    mu[i] <- alpha + alpha2 + beta * x[i]
  }
  alpha  ~ dnorm(0, 5)
  alpha2 ~ dnorm(0, 5)
  beta   ~ dnorm(0, 5)
  sigma  ~ dunif(0, 100)
}
```

Use `Lower_jaw_length` as `y` and `Standard_length` as `x` in FANGS.

Look closely at the mean: `alpha` and `alpha2` *only ever appear added together*.
The data see the sum $\alpha + \alpha_2$ — never the two pieces individually.
Any increase in `alpha` can be exactly cancelled by an equal decrease in `alpha2`
with **no change to the fit**. The likelihood is completely flat along the ridge

$$\alpha + \alpha_2 = \text{constant.}$$

This is a **non-identifiable** parameterisation.

## Part 1 — watch the chains fail

1. Load `fish-lengths.csv` in FANGS, setting `y = Lower_jaw_length` and
   `x = Standard_length`.
2. Paste the two-intercept model above and run with **4 chains**.
3. Open the **Trace** tab and watch `alpha` and `alpha2`.

You should see them **wander** — slow, drifting traces that never settle, looking
different in every chain. Check the **Summary** tab:

- **R-hat** for `alpha` and `alpha2` will be large (well above 1.01).
- **ESS** will be tiny — thousands of iterations buy only a handful of
  effectively independent samples.
- But `beta`, `sigma`, and the *sum* `alpha + alpha2` remain **well-behaved**.
  Only the unidentified pieces misbehave.

::: callout-tip
The data constrain a *line* through $(\alpha, \alpha_2)$ space, not a *point*.
The sampler slides freely along that line — so it does.
:::

## Part 2 — see the bimodality

Open the **Posteriors** tab and look at the density for `alpha` (and `alpha2`).
Because each chain settles in a different stretch of the ridge and mixes badly,
the pooled density often looks **lumpy or multi-modal** — two or more bumps where
you expected one smooth hill. That bimodality is **not a real feature of the
data**; it is an artefact of the non-identified parameter. Compare it with the
density of `beta`, which is a single clean peak.

## Part 3 — let a prior rescue it

A **prior can identify what the data cannot**. Tighten the prior on `alpha2`
step by step, refitting each time:

| Step | Prior on `alpha2`        | What it says |
|------|--------------------------|--------------|
| 1    | `alpha2 ~ dnorm(0, 5)`   | "alpha2 could be almost anything" |
| 2    | `alpha2 ~ dnorm(0, 1)`   | "alpha2 is probably small" |
| 3    | `alpha2 ~ dnorm(0, 0.2)` | "alpha2 is very close to 0" |

As the prior on `alpha2` tightens, it pins `alpha2` near zero, breaking the
symmetry of the ridge. Watch what happens:

- The `alpha` trace **stops wandering** and settles.
- **R-hat falls toward 1.00** and **ESS climbs** by orders of magnitude.
- The lumpy, bimodal density collapses into a **single clean peak**.

You have not added any data. You have simply supplied **external information**
about one parameter — and that was enough to make the whole model sample cleanly.

## The big idea

> A model that looks hopeless to a likelihood-only analysis — because the data
> alone cannot identify every parameter — can be **perfectly usable in a Bayesian
> analysis** when you bring genuine prior information to the unidentified parts.

This comes with responsibility: the result now **depends on the prior** you chose
for `alpha2`. That is fine if the prior reflects real knowledge, and dishonest if
you tightened it just to make the sampler behave. Be transparent about where the
information came from.

## Record your runs

Fit the two-intercept jaw-length model under **at least the three priors on
`alpha2`** in the table above. For each run, record the prior SD, the worst
R-hat of `alpha`/`alpha2`, the minimum ESS, and a quick note on the posterior
shape (*ridge / bimodal / single peak*). Two or more rows unlock the module.
